text: Ladies and gentlemen, this video is aimed at trainees and junior surgeons and will show a step-by-step approach to transanal advancement flap repair for perianal fistulas. This popular type of repair was first described by Arthur Elting from Albany, New York, over a century ago. Interestingly, the flap that was originally described by Elting is quite different from the flap many of us perform. He mobilized the rectal mucosa, but did not make lateral incisions before advancing the rectal mucosa caudally. Even though few randomized data are available, several reviews have concluded that the weighted average healing rate of flap repair is about eighty percent. Furthermore, it is the best investigated procedure for fistulas available, and should therefore be considered the gold standard for the treatment of complex perianal fistulas.

The present video will not cover patient selection and imaging. If you are interested in these subjects visit the ESCP resource library and watch the video on patient selection and the video on MRI imaging of perianal fistulas.

A schematic animation illustrates the anatomy relevant to transanal advancement flap repair, showing the external anal sphincter, internal sphincter, rectal mucosa, and dentate line. A complex trans-sphincteric fistula is depicted, suitable for complex repair procedures.

The surgeon begins the procedure by coring out the external part of the trans-fingleric fistula. The operation proceeds intra-anally with the excision of the internal opening. The remaining fistula is either excised or curetted, resulting in a large defect.

The surgeon creates a thick flap to close the defect, raising it over several centimeters. The internal opening is closed, and the flap is advanced over it, then sutured in place to cover the defect with healthy tissue.

The surgeon begins the core out fistulectomy, using a Lone Star retractor for deeper tracts. A microcautery device, such as the Colorado needle, is employed to remove granulation tissue up to the external sphincter. The cored out fistula is visible, often removed in one piece.

There is some debate whether or not coring out is necessary. A review by the Valencia Group found no difference between core-out and simple curettage. There was however no control of retrospective source data. Many surgeons would advocate core-out. In some patients, in whom minimal separation is found, curettage can however be enough. Remember that the aim is to remove all separation and then choose the best way to achieve this goal.

The surgeon performs a wide excision of the internal opening, creating a large elliptical defect to remove all separated tissue. The excised internal opening is visible, along with the de-epithelialized landing zone prepared for flap attachment. The external wound is temporarily packed to maintain hemostasis before suturing the internal opening.
The surgeon closes the defect at the internal opening using resorbable braided sutures after carefully clearing the intra-sphincteric space. The defect appears large following thorough debridement.

The surgeon creates an advancement flap approximately five centimeters long, with a base wider than its tip. The flap is raised to cover the internal opening, ensuring it includes multiple anatomical layers for improved performance.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to create the advancement flap, ensuring quick cutting and effective hemostasis. The flap is approximately four to five centimeters in length, providing tension-free coverage below the sutured internal opening. A Lone Star retractor, augmented with a disposable plastic retractor, is used to maintain exposure during the procedure.

The surgeon advances and fixates the flap using two barbed sutures, incrementally securing it from proximal to distal. The flap is visibly well-fixed and well-perfused, providing good coverage over the sutured internal opening.

Additional measures worth mentioning are that we operate only patients with anterior fistulas in prone position. Lithotomy is often good enough. We administer single-shot prophylactic antibiotics only. Patients are admitted for one night. We advise against bicycle riding for two to four months after the operation.

Finally, before you commence this operation, ask yourself these questions. Is this operation the best option for your patient at this time? Furthermore, are you the best to perform it? Flap repair is a complicated procedure that is unsuitable for unexperienced hands. It is imperative to have enough exposure to gain this experience and you should ideally be aware of the results of your center. I hope you will find this video useful for your daily practice. If you have any questions or additional comments, please do not hesitate to contact me. Thank you for your attention.
